reasoning: Output B edges out Output A on four dimensions that matter most for contract quality: (1) it explicitly states the contract file path (`docs/contracts/2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md`) in the summary, satisfying expectation 1, which Output A entirely omits; (2) its error cases are more granular — 11 cases vs 8, distinguishing float threshold (ERR-6), whitespace-only category (trimmed empty), and XSS boundary responsibility (ERR-11), demonstrating deeper input analysis; (3) its Error Strategy section is structurally superior, defining an explicit ValidationError class hierarchy with a concrete route handler code pattern, making the layered error handling immediately implementable; (4) its consumer map identifies the alerting worker (`apps/api/src/workers/alertWorker.js`) as a consumer, a cross-system dependency Output A misses entirely. Output A's main advantage is that its blast radius section uses concrete existing line numbers (e.g., `alertConfigService.js:L65`) rather than estimated ranges, and its test assertion skeletons are presented as a dedicated section with more complete coverage. However, Output B's 'likely' line estimates are intellectually honest for a new file that doesn't yet exist, and its inline test skeletons per postcondition are actually more readable. Both outputs are high quality — both pass 9/10 expectations. Output A fails expectation 1 (no docs/contracts/ path stated). Output B's blast radius line references are estimated rather than verified, which is a minor weakness but acceptable given the file is new. On overall contract engineering quality, completeness, and precision, Output B is the stronger artifact.

strengths: Blast radius section uses verified, specific line numbers from existing sibling functions (e.g., syncConfigService.js:L18, notificationRuleService.js:L24)
Dedicated Test Assertion Skeletons section with full runnable Jest code blocks, including the tenant_id injection attack test (PC-X2)
Consumer map includes a 'Separation of concerns check' note explaining why no separate lightweight endpoint is needed
7 NOT in Scope items (most explicit boundary-setting of both outputs)
Error strategy explicitly addresses retry policy with rationale ('INSERT not idempotent — no retry')
INV-7 references the specific migration file name (0042_create_alert_configs.sql)
weaknesses: Does not state the contract file path (docs/contracts/) anywhere — fails expectation 1
Misses alertWorker.js as a consumer — a cross-system blast radius item
Only 8 error cases — misses float threshold, whitespace-only category, and XSS boundary distinction
No explicit ValidationError class defined — error type architecture is implicit

strengths: Explicitly states contract file path in Summary section: docs/contracts/2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md
11 error cases with finer-grained input analysis: distinguishes float (1.5), negative, zero, string, whitespace category separately
ERR-11 XSS boundary case explicitly names the design decision (store verbatim, escaping is UI responsibility) — architecturally important
Error Strategy defines explicit ValidationError vs DatabaseError class hierarchy with concrete route handler code pattern
Consumer map identifies alertWorker.js as a cross-system consumer — catches a blast radius item Output A misses
Blast radius notes 'likely in same file' language is intellectually honest for a new file not yet written
Summary section provides a crisp at-a-glance contract stats block (14 PCs, 11 errors, 5 consumers, etc.)
ValidationError typed throws in service layer are explicitly contracted (PC-S3, PC-S4), making the class hierarchy testable
weaknesses: Blast radius line references for same-file siblings use estimated ranges ('likely L42–L65') rather than verified line numbers — acceptable for a new file but weaker than Output A's verified cross-file references
Test skeletons are embedded inline in PC table cells, making them harder to read for complex assertions vs Output A's dedicated section
